concepts_addressed: Market Saturation Narrative Is Usually a Performance Problem
Warm vs. Cold Sales Process Mismatch

frameworks.name: Core Four Advertising Methods
frameworks.definition: The only four ways a person can let others know about what they sell, derived from combining two audience types (warm/cold) with two communication modes (one-to-one/one-to-many): warm outreach, posting free content, cold outreach, and paid ads.

frameworks.name: Lead Getter Hierarchy
frameworks.definition: Four types of external parties who advertise on your behalf in ascending order of leverage: customers (referrals), employees, agencies, and affiliates; each multiplies lead flow beyond what a single founder can generate.

frameworks.name: More Better New
frameworks.definition: A three-stage framework for scaling any advertising channel: first maximize volume (more), then improve conversion rates at the biggest drop-off constraint (better), then expand to new placements, platforms, or core four activities (new).

frameworks.name: Document-Demonstrate-Duplicate
frameworks.definition: A three-step employee training model: document every step of the task as a checklist while performing it, demonstrate the checklist step-by-step in front of the trainee, then have the trainee duplicate the process while being observed.

frameworks.name: Whisper-Tease-Shout Launch Method
frameworks.definition: A three-phase product and affiliate launch sequence: whisper (create curiosity months out with behind-the-scenes hints), tease (reveal value elements and date weeks out), shout (issue specific CTAs with urgency and scarcity in the final days).

frameworks.name: What-Who-When Ad Framework
frameworks.definition: An advertising copywriting structure that combines the eight value elements (dream outcome, perceived likelihood, time delay, effort) with multiple stakeholder perspectives (prospect, spouse, colleagues, rivals) and three time horizons (past, present, future) to generate compelling ad angles.

frameworks.name: Seven-Step Lead Magnet Creation
frameworks.definition: A sequential process for building a high-converting lead magnet: identify the narrow problem and right avatar, select solution type (reveal problem / sample / one step of multi-step), choose delivery mechanism (software / information / service / physical product), test naming, maximize ease of consumption, maximize quality, and add a clear CTA.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that all advertising reduces to exactly four methods, derived from two binary variables: whether the audience knows you (warm vs. cold) and whether communication is one-to-one or one-to-many. These produce: warm outreach (1-to-1, warm), posting free content (1-to-many, warm), cold outreach (1-to-1, cold), and paid ads (1-to-many, cold). No additional advertising method exists outside this matrix.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that a lead magnet (a free or low-cost solution to a narrow problem) consistently outperforms advertising a core offer directly, because it lowers the perceived cost enough to increase engaged-lead volume by roughly 10x for the same ad spend, cutting customer acquisition cost by approximately 3x. Hormozi illustrates this with a worked example: advertising a core offer directly costs $1,000 per new customer at a 1-in-3 close rate on $3,000 in ad spend; advertising a $25 lead magnet instead costs $100 per qualified call and $1,000 per new customer at 1-in-10 conversion on the lead magnet, tripling the business on the same budget.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: business sells offers requiring more than one-step decision
lead magnet is genuinely valuable and closely related to core offer
claims.mechanism: People are more willing to exchange contact information for free value than to commit to a purchase; the lead magnet pre-qualifies intent and builds trust before the core offer is introduced.
bounded_by: Lead magnet must be darn good (high quality), not sucky fluff; a weak lead magnet destroys reputation
Only applies when the product requires considered purchase; does not apply to impulse or commodity buying

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that the content you create is not the compounding asset in audience building; the audience itself is the compounding asset. Content may disappear over time, but the audience keeps growing. Hormozi held the opposite belief for years and watched Kylie Jenner, Huda Kattan, Conor McGregor, and Dwayne Johnson become billionaires through audience leverage before changing his mind.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: An audience, once built, will see and share future content; each piece distributed to a larger audience compounds total reach without proportional additional effort.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that when he increased his content output by 10x over six months (from minimal posting to maximum output across platforms), his audience grew 10x as fast, adding 1.2 million followers in that period versus 200,000 in the prior 12 months at lower volume. This was observed on his own social media accounts starting around 2020-2021.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: consistent posting across all relevant platforms
content quality maintained or improved
claims.mechanism: Higher posting frequency gives platforms more indexable content to distribute, captures more new-audience discovery events, and compounds algorithmic favor.

numbers.axis: audience growth rate vs. content output

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that 78% of all Gym Launch clients had consumed at least two long-form pieces of his content before booking a sales call, discovered when he surveyed customers after noticing a paid-ads decline in 2020 that could not be fully explained by ad performance. He had attributed all sales to paid ads, but the survey revealed free content was nurturing the majority of demand.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: high-consideration B2B service sale (gym licensing and business services)
audience where trust and credibility matter before purchase
claims.mechanism: Free content pre-warms cold audiences toward purchase intent; without content consumption, paid ad leads arrive with less trust, reducing conversion rates.

numbers.axis: percentage of customers who consumed free content before booking

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that within content creation, audience growth is the only reliable metric of performance: if the audience grows, the content is working; if it does not grow, the content is not rewarding viewers regardless of how good the creator believes it to be. The audience, not the creator, decides whether content is good.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that there is no such thing as content that is too long, only content that is too boring. People will binge long-form content (books, TV series, podcasts) for hours if rewarded. Attention span decline is a false diagnosis; the real change is that audiences have higher standards because they have more rewarding alternatives.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Human biology for sustained attention has not changed; what has changed is the density of competing high-quality content available at any moment.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that the optimal give-to-ask ratio for growing an audience (as opposed to monetizing a mature one) is dramatically higher than what mature platforms like television (~3.5:1 give:ask) or Facebook (~4:1) use. Growing platforms give far more and ask far less. His preferred strategy is give until they ask, meaning he never explicitly asks his audience to buy anything publicly, allowing demand to surface organically through private DMs or email, which produces the highest-quality customers.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: applies to audience building phase, not mature monetization phase
requires long-term patience; short-term revenue is sacrificed for audience compounding
claims.mechanism: Audiences that never see a pitch build higher trust and share content more, compounding audience size; when they finally ask to buy, they are higher-intent customers with lower churn.

anchors.ratio: much higher; give give give, rarely ask

bounded_by: If you need short-term cash flow to pay rent or employees, you must ask sooner; the give-until-they-ask strategy is for operators with enough runway to be patient

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that cold outreach consistently underperforms expectations in the first 3-6 months but becomes a reliable, scalable customer-acquisition machine after approximately 9-12 months of committed operation, because response rates, conversion rates, and list quality all improve with iteration. In his own Gym Launch cold outreach launch (2020), results went from zero sales in September to 30 sales ($480,000 revenue) in May, doubling roughly every 5-6 weeks once momentum began.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: high-ticket B2B service business ($16,000+ per customer)
dedicated team with consistent daily volume
full-cycle tracking of metrics
claims.mechanism: Cold outreach requires learning the right list sources, personalization techniques, follow-up cadences, and scripts; these compound as the team iterates, building toward a reliable lead-to-customer equation.

numbers.axis: monthly sales from cold outreach ramp

bounded_by: Low-ticket direct-to-consumer products are often not profitable with cold outreach teams; Hormozi recommends using cold outreach to recruit affiliates instead in those cases

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that for cold email campaigns targeting niche B2B audiences, a 3% engaged-lead rate (percentage of contacted people who reply showing interest) is a reasonable benchmark. He showed a live example from a portfolio company achieving 4% (48 replies from 801 contacts with 16% open rate), with approximately one-third of engaged leads converting to sales, yielding roughly 1 customer per 100 outreach attempts.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: niche high-ticket service business
personalized cold email (not bulk templated blast)
strong lead magnet or offer in email

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that every business owner who believes they have saturated their market is almost certainly wrong; the apparent saturation reflects insufficient advertising volume and method diversity rather than genuine market exhaustion. He illustrated this with an entrepreneur claiming market saturation at $2M/year in revenue from $30,000/month on a single Facebook platform in the $15.1 billion chiropractic industry, while another entrepreneur in the same niche was spending $30,000 per week across four platforms.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.topic: Market Saturation Narrative Is Usually a Performance Problem
claims.mechanism: Businesses typically use one core four method, on one platform, in one specific targeting configuration; this is a tiny slice of the total addressable audience. Competitors using the same narrow approach appear to fill the visible market, but the vast majority of the market is untouched.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that the LTGP-to-CAC ratio is the primary efficiency metric for paid advertising, and that every portfolio company he has worked with that struggled to scale had an LTGP:CAC ratio below 3:1. When he improved that ratio above 3:1 (either by reducing CAC or increasing LTGP), those companies began to scale. He states this is a personal observation from his Acquisition.com portfolio, not a universal law.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: applies to businesses using paid advertising for customer acquisition
LTGP measured as lifetime gross profit, not lifetime revenue
claims.mechanism: Below 3:1, scaling ad spend increases losses faster than revenue; above 3:1, each dollar of ad spend generates enough gross profit to sustain operations and fund further customer acquisition.

anchors.outcome: business struggles to scale; losing money on acquisition
business takes off; scaling becomes viable

bounded_by: Observation from Acquisition.com portfolio, not a mathematically derived universal rule

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that in paid advertising, the difference between competitors in the same industry in terms of cost to acquire customers (CAC) is much smaller than most entrepreneurs assume; the real differentiator between winning and losing businesses in paid ads is how much they make per customer (LTGP), not how cheaply they acquire them. Lowering CAC has diminishing returns since costs can only approach zero, whereas LTGP can increase infinitely.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: applies to businesses in competitive markets where multiple advertisers target the same audience
claims.mechanism: When multiple advertisers compete for the same audience, auction dynamics converge CAC toward a market equilibrium; the sustainable competitive edge must therefore come from offer quality, upsells, or retention that increase LTGP.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi defines client-financed acquisition as collecting more cash from a customer in the first 30 days than it costs to acquire and fulfill them, which eliminates the need for external capital to scale advertising. He illustrates this with a worked example: a $15/month membership costing $5 to deliver yields $10 gross profit per month, but if a 1-in-5 $100 upsell (100% margin) is added immediately after signup, the average customer delivers $30 gross profit in the first 30 days, exactly covering a $30 CAC.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: 30-day credit card float provides interest-free working capital window
business must be able to offer a meaningful upsell within 30 days of first purchase
claims.mechanism: Any business with a credit card can defer payment 30 days for free; if the customer generates enough cash in those 30 days to cover CAC and COGS, the cycle self-funds: acquire customer, collect cash, pay card, repeat. No outside capital required.

numbers.axis: 30-day cash collected vs. customer acquisition cost

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that for businesses below $1,000,000 per year in profit, the highest-return advertising action is almost always to do more volume first before optimizing conversion rates, because with low absolute volumes, a percentage improvement in conversion rates produces negligible absolute gains. For businesses above $1,000,000 in annual profit, he reverses the priority order to better-more-new, because conversion improvements compound on a large base.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: below $1M/year profit: do more first
above $1M/year profit: make it better first, then more
claims.mechanism: At low volume, percentage-point improvements in conversion produce small absolute gains; at high volume, the same percentage improvement produces large absolute gains that justify optimization effort.

numbers.axis: optimal advertising priority by business revenue

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that referrals from existing customers are superior to any other lead source because referred customers (a) buy more expensive products, (b) purchase more frequently, (c) pay more often in cash upfront, and (d) cost nothing to acquire. He reports that at Gym Launch circa 2019, when paid Facebook ads were accidentally shut off for two weeks, the business was still generating $500,000+ per week in revenue purely from word-of-mouth referrals. At a 700-person Gym Launch event, nearly the entire room of gym owners indicated they had heard about Gym Launch from another gym owner.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: applicable when product delivers enough value that customers are not embarrassed to refer it
requires goodwill (value delivered substantially exceeds price paid)
claims.mechanism: Referred customers arrive with pre-established trust from their referrer; they have lower skepticism, faster decision cycles, and higher willingness to pay because social proof from a peer is more credible than any advertisement.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that most businesses do not get referrals for one of two reasons: their product is not good enough, or they never ask. The price-value-goodwill model states that goodwill equals the gap between the value customers receive and the price they pay; higher goodwill creates word-of-mouth. Businesses can grow goodwill by increasing value delivery across six dimensions: better customer targeting, better expectation setting (under-promise, over-deliver), better results, faster wins, reduced effort and sacrifice, and ongoing CTAs to buy more.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Goodwill is customer surplus (value perceived minus price paid); high goodwill compels customers to refer because they believe their friend will receive a windfall and because the referral enhances the referrer's social status.
bounded_by: Word-of-mouth growth equation requires referral rate to exceed churn rate; businesses losing customers faster than they gain referrals cannot compound through referrals

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that Dropbox's two-sided referral program (500 MB of free storage to referrer and 500 MB to referred friend, up to 16 GB) multiplied their user base by 39x in 15 months. PayPal's two-sided referral program ($10 credit to both referrer and referee) helped them reach 1 million users within 2 years and 100 million users within 6 years of launch.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: viral growth requires that both parties receive incentive aligned with the core product value
referral incentive must be compelling enough to motivate action
claims.mechanism: Two-sided incentives double the motivation to refer; both referrer and referee gain, making the introduction feel like a gift rather than a sales pitch.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that employees transform a founder-dependent business into a sellable asset because a business that requires the founder to operate has near-zero enterprise value to outside buyers. He illustrates this with two scenarios: a $5M revenue, $2M profit business that requires the founder has essentially job-like value, while the same financials in a business that operates without the founder could be worth $10M+ to an acquirer.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: applies to service and advertising-dependent businesses
acquirer valuation assumes a multiple of profit that requires operating independence
claims.mechanism: A business that can run without the founder is a financial asset anyone can own; one that requires the founder is a job, not an asset, and carries key-person risk that acquirers discount heavily or decline entirely.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that the correct way to use advertising agencies is not to outsource advertising indefinitely, but to hire them with an explicit 6-month learning contract where they teach the founder's team their methods, after which the team internalizes the skills and the agency relationship shifts to cheaper consulting only. He states he has repeatedly shortened the time for his team to match or beat agency performance from roughly 12 months to under 6 months with this model.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: applies when a founder wants to build durable internal advertising capability
requires agency to agree to teaching arrangement; some agencies will refuse
claims.mechanism: Agencies rotate their best reps to newest clients; an internally trained team stays fully dedicated to one company and compounds expertise indefinitely, eventually surpassing agency performance on platform-specific optimization.

numbers.axis: time for internal team to match agency performance

bounded_by: Does not apply when the founder never wants to operate the channel internally; some large companies rationally use agencies indefinitely

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that in building an affiliate program, charging affiliates 10-20% of what the average active affiliate earns in the first 12 months as an onboarding and certification fee maximizes the number of affiliates who become active (rather than dormant). Too-low fees attract affiliates who do not follow through; too-high fees reduce total affiliate recruitment. A tiered payout structure (25% of max CAC at signup, 50% upon activation, 100% upon sustained performance) keeps blended payout well below the maximum allowable CAC, improving LTGP:CAC from 3:1 to 4:1 or better.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: applies to affiliate programs where the sponsor company bears fulfillment costs
affiliate business has existing audience that matches the sponsor's customer profile
claims.mechanism: Paying for onboarding creates sunk-cost commitment that increases follow-through; tiered payouts reward only producing affiliates, lowering the blended CAC vs. paying all affiliates the maximum rate.

numbers.axis: affiliate onboarding fee as % of affiliate first-year earnings

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that the Rule of 100 is the minimum advertising commitment for any new channel to produce reliable results: 100 primary actions per day for 100 consecutive days. Below this volume, results are too sparse to distinguish signal from noise and the channel appears not to work when the real problem is insufficient volume. He states that many entrepreneurs believe they have tested a channel after 100 total outreach attempts; the correct baseline is 100 per day.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: applies during initial channel testing and ramp phase
primary action is defined per channel: reach outs, content minutes, ad spend minutes, etc.
claims.mechanism: Low-volume testing produces too few data points to identify patterns; 100 actions per day over 100 days creates enough feedback loops to distinguish channel viability from operator error.

numbers.axis: daily volume commitment for valid channel test

anchors.outcome: statistically insufficient; cannot distinguish signal from noise
reliable engaged leads; valid channel test

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that the three-tier affiliate payout structure (25% of max CAC at entry, 50% upon activation, 100% upon sustained performance) produces a blended payout significantly below the maximum allowable CAC when 60% of sales come from tier-3 affiliates and 20% each from tiers 1 and 2. In his example with a maximum allowable CAC of $40, this blended payout structure yields $30 per acquisition versus $40 maximum, improving LTGP:CAC from 3:1 to 4:1 and potentially adding 10-20% more net profit at year end.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: tiered activation structure in place
60% of affiliate sales come from top-tier activated affiliates
claims.mechanism: Most affiliates produce little volume but are cheap to pay at entry; a small number drive the majority of revenue and earn the full CAC; the blended average is therefore substantially below the maximum, preserving margin.

numbers.axis: blended affiliate payout vs. maximum allowable CAC

claims.text: Alex Hormozi reports that Acquisition.com portfolio companies show the following average results in the first 12 months post-investment: 1.8x revenue growth and 3.01x profit growth; and in months 12-24, 2.3x revenue growth and 4.7x profit growth. These figures represent the average across Acquisition.com's portfolio as of the time of writing (2023).
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Acquisition.com portfolio companies
invested businesses were already $1M+ in annual profit at time of investment
measured as of 2023

numbers.axis: portfolio company revenue and profit growth post-investment

bounded_by: Applies only to Acquisition.com portfolio companies who met investment criteria ($1M+ profit) at time of investment

examples.text: Alex Hormozi describes the March 2017 Gym Launch crisis: he and Leila (then girlfriend and business partner) had $150,000 in unexpected refunds after two gym owners violated their agreements (one told members to demand refunds and leave, another poached clients at a discount). They had no money, no way to sell their way out, and a team of employees to pay. Hormozi stayed up until 3 AM listing options, then pivoted: they ran Facebook ads to a sales page for Leila's online fitness coaching, launched at $499 per client, scaled to $1,000/day within days, then sold their Gym Launch advertising system as a license to gym owners they had planned to visit for $6,000 to $10,000 each. In 30 days they made $215,000 in profit, covering the $150,000 refund gap with cash to spare. This origin story illustrates how advertising knowledge creates a permanent get-out-of-jail-free card.

Alex Hormozi describes his failed webinar attempt in 2016: he joined a $25,000 group whose members advised doing a weekly webinar, spent eight Sundays building a webinar (learning landing pages, thank-you pages, email sequences, software, ad targeting), launched at $150/day in Facebook ads, and got 80 leads and zero sales in 3 days. He then accidentally saw a competitor's ad: a free case study (a 13-minute screen recording walking through an actual advertising campaign with numbers). He rebuilt his lead magnet as a similar case study titled 'FREE Case Study: How we added 213 members and $112,000 in revenue to a small gym in San Diego' directing people to book a call. The next morning Leila's calendar was fully booked with strangers. The case study lead magnet worked because it gave people something they wanted (proof of concept) rather than a webinar they had to commit to watching.

Alex Hormozi describes testing his book title $100M Leads through multiple rounds of social media polls. In headline tests, 'Leads' beat 'Advertising' and 'Marketing' as the subtitle word. In cover image tests, a real photograph beat a cartoon illustration. In subheadline tests, 'How to get strangers to want to buy your stuff' overwhelmingly beat all alternatives including versions with or without 'how to' and with or without the word 'more'. The winning version contained two words ('how to') that the alternatives lacked. He ran these polls publicly and got directional data from hundreds of votes before committing to the final title, illustrating the lead magnet naming step.

Alex Hormozi describes launching Prestige Labs, his supplement company, to gym owner affiliates in December 2018. After over a year of preparation, $1M+ in custom affiliate software and training, and $3M in inventory, the first three weeks produced only $150,000 in sales against $3M in inventory. He feared a catastrophic failure. Then in week four: $100,000 on Monday, $110,000 on Tuesday, $92,000 on Wednesday. They averaged $450,000 that week and sustained $383,000-$452,000 weekly thereafter with 400-444 active affiliates and 2,000+ orders per week. No paid ads, no direct sales team. Illustrates the affiliate launch cycle and the time delay between activation and steady-state performance.

Alex Hormozi describes the cold outreach build at Gym Launch during COVID-19 (2020). Three companies relied entirely on brick-and-mortar gyms staying open; Apple's iOS update had also hurt paid ads. A salesman who had become top producer at a gym software company doing $10M/month from pure cold outreach offered to build a similar system. Results: September: 0 sales. October: 2 sales ($32,000). Team pushed to shut it down. December: 4 sales ($64,000). Team again pushed to shut it down. January: 6 sales. February: 10 sales. March: 14. April: 20. May: 30 sales ($480,000). Today cold outreach generates millions per month. The example illustrates the long ramp-up of cold outreach and the importance of not shutting it down prematurely.

Alex Hormozi describes how a PR portfolio company had plateaued for years despite high sales volume because its lowest-churning customers (only 15% of the business) were a specific niche seeking to raise investor funding. When the founder switched all advertising callouts to target only this niche, the business broke its plateau, grew for the first time in years, became on track to add millions per month, and saw lower advertising costs because narrower targeting produced cheaper and better-qualified leads who got more value from the product and referred with clockwork regularity.

Alex Hormozi describes a personal hiring lesson: he tried cold outreach twice before succeeding. The first attempt used someone external with outreach experience who failed. The second used an internal person with no outreach experience who also failed. The third used an internal person with cold outreach experience who succeeded and scaled the channel to millions per month. The key variable in all three attempts was not the method but the person hired to lead it, illustrating why experience (not potential) matters for high-complexity advertising channels.

Alex Hormozi describes a flyer campaign story from 2014: he printed 300 flyers and distributed them on cars near his gym, following a mentor's advice. He received one call, from a person whose Mercedes had been scratched, and no leads. He called his mentor to complain. The mentor responded: he tests with 5,000 flyers, then distributes 5,000 per day every day for a month when he finds a winner. At 0.5% response rate, 300 flyers would yield 1.5 responses statistically. The lesson: most entrepreneurs do 1/1,500th the required effort then conclude the method does not work.

practices.text: Reach out to 100 contacts per day for warm outreach, using personalized messages, following up up to three times per contact across multiple platforms (email, phone, DM, SMS, voicemail).
When starting a new product or service, make the first five deliveries free to get reps, collect feedback, build testimonials, and identify hidden costs before charging.
Ask for referrals at the moment of purchase, not later; include a three-way introduction request rather than just a name-and-number handoff.
Use only one call-to-action per content piece or ad; a confused mind does not buy, so do not ask audiences to simultaneously like, share, subscribe, and comment.
Before spending on paid ads, set up full tracking so you can accurately measure return on every dollar; launching without tracking is equivalent to playing a casino game without a budget.
Test one variable per week per advertising platform, log all test results, and only move to the next variable after four failed attempts to beat the current control on the identified constraint.
Give employees a finite, manageable lead quota each week (e.g., 70 leads for a rep who could handle 100) so they can work each lead thoroughly rather than burning through a large list carelessly.
Deliver progress updates and small wins as frequently as possible within the first 48 hours after a customer buys; customers form their lasting impression of a business in this window.
Structure cold outreach so that if someone asks you not to contact them again, remove them immediately; it is more efficient to convert neutrals to yes than negatives to yes.
Make content assuming the viewer has never heard of you; inside jokes and internal references slow audience growth because strangers feel excluded and will not share.
